RULES:

  origins. If the Evidence you were granted does not settle a probe, answer UNCERTAIN.

=== EVIDENCE (your partition: FULL — Sees everything (both abstracts + all four extracted claims). Highest information, but shared with every other FULL assessor — the correlated baseline.) ===
[SOURCE] The Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market in Wuhan was the early epicenter of the COVID-19 pandemic
         Worobey M, et al. (18 authors). Science 2022. doi:10.1126/science.abp8715
         abstract (version of record): "Understanding how severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) emerged in 2019 is critical to preventing future zoonotic outbreaks before they become the next pandemic. The Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market in Wuhan, China, was identified as a likely source of cases in early reports, but later this conclusion became controversial. We show here that the earliest known COVID-19 cases from December 2019, including those without reported direct links, were geographically centered on this market. We report that live SARS-CoV-2-susceptible mammals were sold at the market in late 2019 and that within the market, SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples were spatially associated with vendors selling live mammals. Although there is insufficient evidence to define upstream events, and exact circumstances remain obscure, our analyses indicate that the emergence of SARS-CoV-2 occurred through the live wildlife trade in China and show that the Huanan market was the epicenter of the COVID-19 pandemic."
[SOURCE] The molecular epidemiology of multiple zoonotic origins of SARS-CoV-2
         Pekar JE, et al. (29 authors). Science 2022. doi:10.1126/science.abp8337
         abstract (version of record): "Understanding the circumstances that lead to pandemics is important for their prevention. We analyzed the genomic diversity of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) early in the coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic. We show that SARS-CoV-2 genomic diversity before February 2020 likely comprised only two distinct viral lineages, denoted "A" and "B." Phylodynamic rooting methods, coupled with epidemic simulations, reveal that these lineages were the result of at least two separate cross-species transmission events into humans. The first zoonotic transmission likely involved lineage B viruses around 18 November 2019 (23 October to 8 December), and the separate introduction of lineage A likely occurred within weeks of this event. These findings indicate that it is unlikely that SARS-CoV-2 circulated widely in humans before November 2019 and define the narrow window between when SARS-CoV-2 first jumped into humans and when the first cases of COVID-19 were reported. As with other coronaviruses, SARS-CoV-2 emergence likely resulted from multiple zoonotic events."
[CLAIM/L5] The earliest known December-2019 COVID-19 cases were geographically centered on the Huanan market, including cases with no reported direct market link.
[CLAIM/L4] Within the Huanan market, SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples were spatially associated with stalls selling live mammals.
[CLAIM/L5] Live SARS-CoV-2-susceptible mammals were sold at the Huanan market in late 2019.
[CLAIM/L4] SARS-CoV-2's early genomic diversity comprised two distinct lineages (A and B) that resulted from at least two separate zoonotic (cross-species) transmission events into humans.

=== PROBES ===
F1. Worobey et al. (2022) state that the earliest known December-2019 COVID-19 cases — including some without reported direct market links — were geographically centered on the Huanan market.
F2. Worobey et al. (2022) report that live SARS-CoV-2-susceptible mammals were sold at the Huanan market in late 2019.
F3. Worobey et al. (2022) report that SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples inside the market were spatially associated with vendors selling live mammals.
F4. Worobey et al. (2022) report that they had sufficient evidence to define the upstream events — the specific animal source — of SARS-CoV-2.
F5. Pekar et al. (2022) conclude that SARS-CoV-2's early genomic diversity comprised two distinct lineages (A and B) arising from at least two separate cross-species transmission events.
F6. Pekar et al. (2022) estimate the first (lineage B) zoonotic transmission occurred around mid-to-late November 2019.
F7. Pekar et al. (2022) conclude that SARS-CoV-2 was already circulating widely in humans well before November 2019.
F8. The corpus sources report direct genomic sequencing that identifies the specific intermediate animal host from which SARS-CoV-2 spilled over.
I1. On balance, the available corpus evidence supports the Huanan market as the epicenter of the earliest documented COVID-19 outbreak.
I2. On balance, the available corpus evidence makes zoonotic (animal-to-human) spillover a more probable explanation for SARS-CoV-2's emergence than a non-zoonotic introduction.
I3. The three Worobey proximity findings (geographic centering, live-mammal sales, environmental-sample association) are statistically INDEPENDENT lines of evidence whose likelihood ratios may legitimately be multiplied.
I4. The Pekar molecular two-lineage finding rests on data distinct from Worobey's market-proximity findings, so the two may legitimately be treated as independent and combined.
I5. The corpus justifies reporting a single confident point-estimate odds ratio (on the order of 100:1) for zoonosis versus the alternative.
I6. The corpus evidence is sufficient to definitively rule out any non-market introduction of SARS-CoV-2 into humans.
